channel.title: Jung Yeon Park on Jung Yeon Park
channel.description: Recent content in Jung Yeon Park on Jung Yeon Park

item.title: Kubeadm Init
item.description:
This is the last of three posts will focus on how to bring up a kubeadm cluster in an air-gapped environment.

  1. Prepare for kubernetes
  2. Download required images for kubeadm
  3. Initialize Kubeadm [*]

This post focuses on initializing kubeadm, install overlay networking, and join nodes to the initialized cluster.

You should have:

  • Five VMs (1 for master, 4 for workers) and prepared them for a kubeadm install
  • Downloaded the required docker images
  • Uploaded the images to a private repository

Please refer to the first for information on how to set up k8s nodes and the second to download required images from the internet.

1. Kubeadm init

1.1 config file

Kubeadm initialized a cluster with the command kubeadm init. We will use a config file to pass certain options to this command. Note that this config file is still considered alpha, but it worked well in my case. (https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/setup-tools/kubeadm/kubeadm-init/)

cat > kubeadm_init_config.yaml <<EOF
apiVersion: kubeadm.k8s.io/v1alpha1
kind: MasterConfiguration
api:
  advertiseAddress: "192.168.0.1"
networking:
  # Flannel
  podSubnet: 10.244.0.0/16
kubernetesVersion: "v1.9.1"
imageRepository: "<private-registry>:<port>/<repo-name>"
EOF

In the config file, I added the master k8s01 IP: 192.168.0.1 to advertiseAddress. You may not need this option.

For overlay networking, I chose flannel as it was the simplest to deploy. As an aside, if I were to start over on my cluster, I might have gone with calico for its rich support of Network Policies. You can use any other pod network add-on such as calico, canal, or weave net. If you choose flannel make sure to set the podSubnet to 10.244.0.0/16.

Set imageRepository to use the private container registry.

1.2 Pause container

You should have noticed that we downloaded an image named pause in the previous post. Pause containers are used to set up the network namespaces for pods and serves as the “parent container” for all containers of a pod. Please see this article for an in-depth discussion of what it exactly does.

The default location of pause container image is set in /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf as gcr.io/google_containers/pause-amd64:3.0. To use a private image repository, you need to edit the systemd unit file, or add another one as

[Service]
Environment="KUBELET_EXTRA_ARGS=--pod-infra-container-image=<your-image>"

See issue 257.

I decided to preload the pause images on all nodes instead of creating a new systemd file, but you might want to consider this option if you have many nodes.

Run the command on all 5 nodes:

docker load -i pause-amd64_3.0.tar

1.3 Add k8s admin user

For administering your k8s cluster with kubectl, you probably want to use a regular user for security reasons.

Create a new user and set password:

useradd k8sadmin
passwd k8sadmin

To control docker with this user, add it to the docker group and restart docker:

usermod -aG docker k8sadmin
systemctl restart docker

1.4 Kubeadm init

Using the init config file from #1, we will now initialize our kubeadm master. kubeadm init first checks that the k8s master is ready to run Kubernetes and then downloads control plane images. Make sure that you have prepared the nodes as outlined in the first post.

On k8s01 as root:

kubeadm init --config kubeadm_init_config.yaml

If you were successful, it should give you some commands to run for your regular k8sadmin user. Follow these commands.

Your Kubernetes master has initialized successfully!

To start using your cluster, you need to run (as a regular user):

  mkdir -p $HOME/.kube
  sudo cp -i /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
  sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config

You can now run kubectl with the k8sadmin user on k8s01. We will use k8sadmin throughout for kubectl.

kubectl get nodes

The nodes are still not ready as we need to install a pod network.

2. Flannel

To install flannel, you can follow the instructions here. Consult flannel’s github repository for more detailed information.

2.1 YAML

Flannel is installed like any other kubernetes component, using a yaml file. Yaml files describe kubernetes objects. The file is here. You can see that it installs some RBAC components and installs flannel as a DaemonSet. Copy the yaml file onto k8s01. Make sure to only copy the parts for amd64 in DaemonSet.

As I will mainly use k8sadmin to administer my cluster, I created a yaml directory in the home folder and organized yaml files there.

mkdir -p $HOME/yaml/flannel

Open flannel.yaml and check which images are required. Simply look for the image directive. Sometimes, the image directive is split into repository and tag.

image: quay.io/coreos/flannel:v0.10.0-amd64

This tells you which images you need to download from the internet and upload to our private registry.

You should also look over this yaml file and try to understand what each directive means. For starters, the --- allows you to concatenate several yaml files as one. kind tells you the kind of object the yaml is referring to, metadata.name names the object, and metadata.namespace specifies which namespace. The spec format specifies options or fields for that particular kind of object. Please see https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/kubernetes-objects/ for more details.

2.2 Download images

Following the example in the second post, pull the required image, save it as a .tar file and upload to the private registry.

docker pull quay.io/coreos/flannel:v0.10.0-amd64
docker save quay.io/coreos/flannel:v0.10.0-amd64 -o flannel_v0.10.0-amd64.tar

Upload to the private network and push to private registry.

docker load -i flannel_v0.10.0-amd64.tar
docker tag quay.io/coreos/flannel:v0.10.0-amd64 <private-registry>:<port>/flannel/flannel:v0.10.0-amd64
docker push <private-registry>:<port>/flannel/flannel:v0.10.0-amd64

2.3 Edit YAML

Go back to the flannel.yaml and edit the image directive to look to your private registry. You may not need the full url as we edited the docker daemon.json to automatically look first in our private registry, but I always add it just in case.

image: <private-registry>:<port>/flannel:v0.10.0-amd64

2.4 Comments

The bad thing about an air-gapped environment is that you will repeat the above process many, many times, especially for installing complex components (i.e. Prometheus or EFK) that require multiple images and yaml files. Helm, which I will discuss in another post, doesn’t really help with this process as you still need to download the images and edit other files, which is a pain. This is the reason I created simple scripts to get over this process as quickly as possible.

2.5 Install Flannel

Installing flannel is one command:

kubectl apply -f flannel.yaml

Note that we installed flannel in the kube-system namespace as described flannel.yaml. Namespaces separate our k8s cluster as multiple virtual clusters. I usually use kube-system for system components. You can check that the DaemonSet is deployed properly with:

kubectl -n kube-system get daemonset

It should be in the Running state.

3. Join nodes

3.1 Pause image

To join nodes to k8s01, you need to preload the pause images.

docker load -i pause-amd64_3.0.tar

3.2 Join nodes

After kubeadm init you should have seen this in the output:

You can now join any number of machines by running the following on each node
as root:

  kubeadm join --token <token> <master-ip>:<master-port> --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash>

Run this command on all nodes.

3.3 If token expired

If the token expired, or you lost the output, create a new token on k8s01 as root

kubeadm token create --print-join-command
kubeadm token list

This should print the join command again.

3.4 Check that nodes have joined

On k8s01 as kube admin,

kubectl get nodes

They should all be in the Ready state.

item.title: 2. Required images for kubeadm
item.description:
This is the second of three posts will focus on how to bring up a kubeadm cluster in an offline environment. Refer to the first post for information on how to set up k8s nodes and to install kubeadm.

  1. Prepare for kubernetes
  2. Download required images for kubeadm [*]
  3. Initialize Kubeadm

This post focuses on the required Docker images for kubeadm and downloading/uploading them to our private registry.

1. Download docker images from an internet connected computer

First, install docker on an internet connected computer. I installed Virtualbox and booted up a CentOS 7 VM and installed docker as described on their website.

The required images for kubeadm are listed here. I have copied the table over here for reference.

Image Name v1.9 release branch version
k8s.gcr.io/kube-apiserver-${ARCH} v1.9.x
k8s.gcr.io/kube-controller-manager-${ARCH} v1.9.x
k8s.gcr.io/kube-scheduler-${ARCH} v1.9.x
k8s.gcr.io/kube-proxy-${ARCH} v1.9.x
k8s.gcr.io/etcd-${ARCH} 3.1.10
gcr.io/google_containers/pause-${ARCH} 3.0
k8s.gcr.io/k8s-dns-sidecar-${ARCH} 1.14.7
k8s.gcr.io/k8s-dns-kube-dns-${ARCH} 1.14.7
k8s.gcr.io/k8s-dns-dnsmasq-nanny-${ARCH} 1.14.7

I created a short python script to read images from a list, save them as tarballs in the specified output directory. It’s very basic but works well enough.

save.images.py

#!/usr/bin/env python

# arg1: image list file
# arg2: tarball output directory

import os, sys, re, subprocess

if len(sys.argv[1]) > 1:
  image_list_file = sys.argv[1]

rootdir = ""
if len(sys.argv[2]) > 1:
  rootdir = os.path.abspath(sys.argv[2])

if not os.path.exists(rootdir):
  os.makedirs(rootdir)

with open(image_list_file) as file:
  data = [line.strip() for line in file.readlines() if line.strip()]

for line in data:
  image = re.split('[/:]', line)
  tar_name = os.path.join(rootdir, image[-2] + "_" + image[-1] + ".tar")
  if not os.path.exists(tar_name):
    subprocess.call(["docker", "pull", line])
    subprocess.call(["docker", "save", line, "-o", tar_name])
    subprocess.call(["docker", "rmi", line])
    print("\n")
  else:
    print("\n### " + line + " already exists. Skipping ...\n")

Here is the exact image list I used. There shouldn’t be any spaces or empty lines in this file.

image_list

k8s.gcr.io/kube-apiserver-amd64:v1.9.1
k8s.gcr.io/kube-controller-manager-amd64:v1.9.1
k8s.gcr.io/kube-scheduler-amd64:v1.9.1
k8s.gcr.io/kube-proxy-amd64:v1.9.1
k8s.gcr.io/etcd-amd64:3.1.10
k8s.gcr.io/pause-amd64:3.0
k8s.gcr.io/k8s-dns-sidecar-amd64:1.14.7
k8s.gcr.io/k8s-dns-kube-dns-amd64:1.14.7
k8s.gcr.io/k8s-dns-dnsmasq-nanny-amd64:1.14.7

Usage:

./save_images.py image_list output/

And the script will download an image named repo1/repo2/image-name:v1 as image-name_v1.tar inside output/.

2. Copy the saved images to offline environment

Here, you need to have a way to upload the images to the offline environment. I created a FTP server, uploaded the tar files to the FTP server, then downloaded the images to another computer on the same network as the k8s nodes. You can use the master node k8s01 if you wish.

3. Upload images to private registry

To upload Docker images to a private registry, you need to first load the images, then re-tag them, and then push to your private registry.

Ex) Tar file named kube-apiserver-amd64_v1.9.1.tar and image is k8s.gcr.io/kube-apiserver-amd64:v1.9.1.

docker load -i kube-apiserver-amd64_v1.9.1.tar

You can then check that the image is loaded properly and note that the docker image is tagged with the original repository name.

docker images

Re-tag the image to upload to another repository. You will see two images in docker images, but both reference the same image. Substitute <registry-url>:<port> and <repo-name> to your private registry and your desired repo name (I used kubernetes). I kept the image and version the same.

docker tag k8s.gcr.io/kube-apiserver-amd64:v1.9.1 <registry-url>:<port>/kubernetes/kube-apiserver-amd64:v1.9.1

Push to your private registry. Login with your registry credentials if needed.

docker push <registry-url>:<port>/kubernetes/kube-apiserver-amd64:v1.9.1

I again created two short python scripts, the first (load_images.py) to load images and the second (push_images.py) to push images.

load_images.py first deletes all loaded Docker images, and then loads all tarballs in the specified directory (arg1). It deletes all loaded images to be used in conjuction with push_images.py, which retags and pushes every loaded image.

load_images.py

#!/usr/bin/env python3

# arg1: tarball source directory

import subprocess
import os
import sys

print("Deleting docker images...")

subprocess.call('docker rmi -f $(docker images -q)', shell=True)

dir = "."
if len(sys.argv) > 1:
    dir = sys.argv[1]
print("Directory: " + dir)

for filename in os.listdir(dir):
    if ".tar" in filename:
        subprocess.call('docker load -i ' + dir + filename, shell=True)
        print("Loaded " + filename)

push_images.py re-tags all images in your docker list, and pushes them to the private image repository, specifed by arg1. Change <registry-url>:<port> to your private registry.

push_images.py

#!/usr/bin/env python3

# arg1: repository name

import sys, re
import subprocess

private_repo = "<registry-url>:<port>"

if len(sys.argv) < 2 :
  print("Need one argument: repository name. Exiting...")
  exit()

subprocess.call(['docker', 'login', private_repo])

repo = sys.argv[1] if not sys.argv[1].endswith("/") else sys.argv[1][:-1]

output = subprocess.check_output(['docker', 'images', '--format', "{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}}"], universal_newlines=True)
image_list = [i for i in output.split('\n') if i]

for image in image_list:
    if image.startswith(private_repo):
        continue
    fields = re.split('[/:]', image)
    print(fields)
    image_url = "{0}/{1}/{2}:{3}".format(private_repo, repo, fields[-2], fields[-1])
    subprocess.call(['docker', 'tag', image, image_url])
    subprocess.call(['docker', 'push', image_url])
    print("------ Pushed {} to {}.".format(image, image_url))

That’s it for this post. In the next post, I will finish up how to start up a 5-node kubernetes cluster in an offline environment.

item.title: 1. Prepare offline kubeadm install
item.description:
This is the first of three posts will focus on how to bring up a kubeadm cluster in an offline environment. I completed this a couple of months ago, when the latest k8s version was 1.9, but the concepts should translate over to newer versions.

  1. Prepare for kubernetes [*]
  2. Download required images for kubeadm
  3. Initialize Kubeadm

Kubeadm is one of the tools used to create a Kubernetes cluster. There are many different ways to set up a k8s cluster such as minikube, kops, cloud solutions, and so on. I chose kubeadm as it was one of the simplest solutions for an offline install. I would have liked to use kubespray but it requires internet access.

This post mostly focuses on how to prepare nodes for a kubadm installation. I mostly followed the steps here.

I created a single-master cluster with 5 nodes as this was a test cluster. You can configure HA masters by following this guide.

For reference, here is the simplified Kubernetes architecture (from Wikipedia)

Note: In this guide, we will use root for all commands unless explicitly stated.

Some terminology follows:

  • docker: the container runtime we will use. Kubernetes can use others as well.
  • kubeadm: the administration tool to bootstrap a kubernetes cluster
  • kubelet: the kubernetes daemon that runs on all cluster nodes
  • kubectl: a command line interface to interact with a kubernetes cluster

1. Prerequisites

1.1 Kubernetes VMs

I created five VMs with the following specs:

  • 4 vCPUs
  • 16GB RAM (initially used 8GB)
  • 100GB Disk
  • 1 NIC (disconnected from Internet)
  • OS: CentOS 7.3

The master and workers will have the same specs.

1.2 Private repositories

For an easy offline install, I recommend you to set up a private RPM repository for docker and kubernetes repos. You can just copy over the rpm or deb files across all nodes, but it seems like a hassle.

I used Nexus OSS and used reposync/createrepo from an internet connected computer. You can use whatever solution you like, even a simple httpd server will do.

1.3 Private Docker Registry

Similarly, I recommend setting up a private docker registry for all the docker images. You could just save/load all the k8s images, but you will need a registry anyway when deploying applications to Kubernetes.

I used Gitlab CE as it was an easy rpm install. You can use Docker Registry but you need to set it up after installing Docker.

VMware Harbor seems very interesting, and has a nice UI as well.

Here, I will use a insecure docker registry, and is not recommended for production.

1.4 Other requirements

Please view this for all other requirements.

In particular, all nodes must have unique hostnames, MAC addresses, and product_uuids.

2. Node settings

2.1 Disable swap

Kubeadm requires swap to be disabled.

  • To disable temporarily, swapoff -a

  • To disable permanently, edit /etc/fstab and comment the line with swap.

2.2 Firewall ports

Kubernetes requires specific ports to operate: see here

Configure your firewall to allow these inbound/outbound rules, or disable firewall as I did. This is NOT RECOMMENDED FOR PRODUCTION.

systemctl stop firewalld && systemctl disable firewalld

2.3 Configure default route

The VMs I created use a private network, and doesn’t have a default gateway. Kubernetes uses default routes to contact other components, so I simply configured my nodes to have the default gateway as their own IP addresses.

You can edit ifcfg files or use NetworkManager like I did.

2.4 Disable SELinux

Kubernetes requires disabling SELinux to allow containers to access the host filesystem.

  • To disable temporarily, setenforce 0

  • To disable permanently

    sed -i 's/SELINUX=enforcing/SELINUX=disabled/g' /etc/selinux/config

You need to reboot for SELinux changes to take effect. We will reboot after all nodes are configured.

2.5 Create k8s admin user on master node

I created a new user k8sadmin on the master node k8s01 to run kubectl commands.

useradd k8sadmin
passwd k8sadmin

3. Install Docker

Docker 17.03 is recommended for use with Kubernetes.

3.1. Create local docker RPM repository

I followed the instructions from Docker’s website but mirrored their centos repository and uploaded it to my private Nexus repository.

You can browse through their repo for Centos 7 x86_64, and use the ones you want.

Create a repo file for our private docker repository and install docker-ce

cat << EOF > /etc/yum.repos.d/docker-ce.repo
[docker-ce-stable]
name=Docker CE Stable-$basearch
baseurl=<docker-repo-url>
enabled=1
gpgcheck=0
EOF

yum install -y docker-ce-selinux docker-ce
systemctl enable docker && systemctl start docker

I disabled the GPG key here, but is recommended for production.

3.2 Configure docker options

You need to configure docker to use the private docker registry as the default.

Create a /etc/docker/daemon.json file.

cat << EOF > /etc/docker/daemon.json
{
  "registry-mirrors": ["http://<docker-registry-host>:<port>"],
  "insecure-registries": ["<docker-registry-host>:<port>"]
}
EOF

For some reason, I had to add the http:// prefix to registry-mirrors and not to insecure-registries to get it working properly. registry-mirrors allows docker to pull from this mirror before pulling from the public docker Hub, and insecure-registries is for http access.

In 1.9, Kubernetes recommended using systemd as the cgroup-driver but newer versions don’t seem to require it. Consult the latest kubeadm documentation for more info.

Add cgroup-driver option to the daemon.json file and restart docker.

{
  "exec-opts": ["native.cgroupdriver=systemd"],
  "registry-mirrors": ["http://<docker-registry-host>:<port>"],
  "insecure-registries": ["<docker-registry-host>:<port>"]
}

systemctl restart docker

3.3 Copy docker registry credentials if needed

If your registry requires login for pulling images, you need to copy those credentials to all k8s nodes.

First login to the registry

docker login <docker-registry-host>:<port>
Username: jungy.park
Password:
Login Succeeded

Credentials are created in $HOME/.docker/config.json. Copy that file to /root/.docker/config.json to other nodes, so that they can pull images automatically when joining to the kubernetes cluster.

3.4 Non-root user

If you want to use a non-root user to control docker, add that user to the group docker

usermod -aG docker k8sadmin

4. Install kubeadm, kubelet, and kubectl

Make sure that you have created a private kubernetes repository by downloading from the official kubernetes site. Use reposync/createrepo.

4.1 Add kubernetes repo file

cat << EOF > /etc/yum.repos.d/kubernetes.repo
[kubernetes]
name=Kubernetes
baseurl=<local-kubernetes-repo-url>
enabled=1
gpgcheck=0
EOF
yum install -y kubelet kubeadm kubectl
systemctl enable kubelet && systemctl start kubelet

The kubelet should be in a crashloop, until you initialize the master (will cover this in next few posts.)

4.2 Add kernel parameters

For RHEL/CentOS 7, you need to add the following kernel parameters:

cat << EOF > /etc/sysctl.d/10-k8s.conf
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-ip6tables = 1
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables = 1
EOF
sysctl --system

5. Miscellaneous

I manually added the nodes to the hosts file, and copied them across all nodes

cat << EOF > /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1   localhost localhost.localdomain localhost4 localhost4.localdomain4
::1         localhost localhost.localdomain localhost6 localhost6.localdomain6

# Kubernetes Master
192.168.0.1 k8s01

# Kubernetes Nodes
192.168.0.2 k8s02
192.168.0.3 k8s03
192.168.0.4 k8s04
192.168.0.5 k8s05
EOF

Reboot to make sure all changes take effect.

That’s it for this post. In the next, I will talk about how to download and upload docker images and list which were required for initializing a k8s cluster.

item.title: Setting up Minikube with corporate proxy
item.description:
Minikube is an easy way to get started with Kubernetes. It runs as a single-node Kubernetes cluster inside a VM, and includes almost all features of k8s, so you can try them out before installing them to an actual cluster.

Supported addons include:

  • Kubernetes dashboard
  • EFK
  • Registry
  • Ingress

and so on.

(see https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/blob/master/docs/addons.md)

Minikube creates a VM that includes Kubernetes and a Docker daemon. To use containers inside this VM, you need to pass on the proxy settings to the docker daemon and install the certificate as well.

This post focuses on using Minikube with a corporate proxy and certificate. I tested this several months ago so the exact commands may be slightly different; the overall flow should be the same.

I created an Ubuntu host and installed minikube there. I needed to setup proxy settings for the host, and then configure proxy and certificates inside the minikube VM.

1. Host proxy settings

  • Used Ubuntu 16.04 with Desktop

  • Set proxy system-wide

    • System Settings -> Network Settings
    • Enter :
    • May need to add https proxy as well
    • Click apply system-wide

  • Set up no-proxy settings

    • Used dconf-editor (may need to install apt install dconf-editor)
    • Go to System -> Proxy

    • Add line to ignore-hosts:

      ['localhost','127.0.0.1/8','::1','192.168.99.100']

    • 192.168.99.100 is the default IP for minikube

Here I edited the Ubuntu host proxy settings. We add the no-proxy setting to access minikube later on. You can probably edit /etc/environment for the same effect.

2. Update certificates on host

  • Make a new directory under /usr/share/ca-certificates

    sudo mkdir -p /usr/share/ca-certificates/temp

  • Copy your certificate to the new directory

    sudo cp client.crt /usr/share/ca-certificates/temp/

  • Update certificates

    sudo update-ca-certificates

3. Start Minikube

  • Assume virtualbox, kubectl, minikube is installed
    • When installing, you may need to pass proxy settings to curl. Create a .curlrc file if needed

  • Pass proxy settings to docker daemon when starting minikube

    minikube start --docker-env HTTP_PROXY=<proxy> --docker-env HTTPS_PROXY=<proxy> --docker-env NO_PROXY=localhost,127.0.0.1,::1,192.168.99.100 --insecure-registry gcr.io, index.docker.io
    • I had to pass the --insecure-registry parameter to download images correctly. Add any other registries as needed.

  • After minikube has started, check that the docker options have been passed correctly

    minikube ssh -- systemctl show --property=Environment docker --no-pager

4. Pass the certificate to the Minikube VM

  • To properly download images without certificate warnings inside Minikube, you need to copy the certificate to the VM inside Minkube.
  • Copy certificate contents
  • SSH into Minikube minikube ssh
  • Append certificate contents to ca-certificates.crt

  • Restart docker

    sudo systemctl restart docker

  • I created a script copy-crt.sh to do this easily outside the Minikube VM

    #!/usr/bin/env bash
CRT=$(< /usr/share/ca-certificates/temp/client.crt)

minikube ssh << EOF

echo -e "$CRT" | sudo tee -a /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt

sudo systemctl restart docker

exit

EOF

  • The certificate needs to be copied everytime minikube is restarted.

Now you can download images and create applications to test Kubernetes features.

item.title: Models of Clustered Photolithography Tools for Fab-Level Simulation: From Affine to Flow Line
Exit Recursion Models of Clustered Photolithography Tools for Fab Level Simulation
Equipment Models of Clustered Photolithography Tools for Fab-Level Simulation
